title: Hello, World!
learning_objective: Define a function that returns a fixed String value.
stub_code:
module HelloWorld where

greet :: String
greet = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import HelloWorld

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "returns Hello, World!" (greet) ("Hello, World!")
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module HelloWorld where

greet :: String
greet = "Hello, World!"
hints: A string literal in Haskell is written with double quotes.
Replace undefined with the string value the function should return.
Return the string "Hello, World!" exactly — note the comma and exclamation mark.

title: Add Two Integers
learning_objective: Define a function that takes two Int arguments and returns their sum.
stub_code:
module AddIntegers where

add :: Int -> Int -> Int
add x y = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import AddIntegers

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "adds two positive numbers" (add 2 3) (5)
    , assertEqual "adds zero" (add 0 7) (7)
    , assertEqual "adds negative numbers" (add (-4) 1) ((-3))
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module AddIntegers where

add :: Int -> Int -> Int
add x y = x + y
hints: Functions in Haskell return the value of the last expression in their body.
The + operator works on Int values.
Replace undefined with an expression that combines x and y.

title: Concatenate Strings
learning_objective: Build a String by concatenating multiple string values using ++.
stub_code:
module StringConcatenation where

greetName :: String -> String
greetName name = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import StringConcatenation

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "greets Alice" (greetName "Alice") ("Hello, Alice!")
    , assertEqual "greets World" (greetName "World") ("Hello, World!")
    , assertEqual "greets an empty string" (greetName "") ("Hello, !")
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module StringConcatenation where

greetName :: String -> String
greetName name = "Hello, " ++ name ++ "!"
hints: Strings in Haskell can be joined with the ++ operator.
You can chain multiple ++ operations: "a" ++ "b" ++ "c" gives "abc".
Join "Hello, ", the name argument, and "!" using ++.

title: Boolean Comparison
learning_objective: Use a comparison operator to return a Bool value.
stub_code:
module BooleanValues where

isAdult :: Int -> Bool
isAdult age = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import BooleanValues

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "returns True for age 18" (isAdult 18) (True)
    , assertEqual "returns True for age 21" (isAdult 21) (True)
    , assertEqual "returns False for age 17" (isAdult 17) (False)
    , assertEqual "returns False for age 0" (isAdult 0) (False)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module BooleanValues where

isAdult :: Int -> Bool
isAdult age = age >= 18
hints: Haskell comparison operators include <, >, <=, >=, == and /=.
A comparison expression evaluates to a Bool: True or False.
Use >= to check whether age is at least 18.

title: If Expressions
learning_objective: Use if/then/else as an expression that returns a value.
stub_code:
module IfExpressions where

absoluteValue :: Int -> Int
absoluteValue n = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import IfExpressions

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "returns a positive number unchanged" (absoluteValue 5) (5)
    , assertEqual "negates a negative number" (absoluteValue (-3)) (3)
    , assertEqual "returns 0 for 0" (absoluteValue 0) (0)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module IfExpressions where

absoluteValue :: Int -> Int
absoluteValue n = if n < 0 then -n else n
hints: In Haskell, if/then/else is an expression — both branches must return the same type.
The syntax is: if <condition> then <value-if-true> else <value-if-false>
To negate n, write -n. You may need parentheses when passing it to functions: (-n).

title: Lambda Functions
learning_objective: Write an anonymous function using backslash lambda syntax.
stub_code:
module LambdaFunction where

-- Implement double using a lambda expression
double :: Int -> Int
double = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import LambdaFunction

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "double 3" (double 3) 6
    , assertEqual "double 0" (double 0) 0
    , assertEqual "double (-2)" (double (-2)) (-4)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module LambdaFunction where

double :: Int -> Int
double = \x -> x * 2
hints: Lambda syntax in Haskell: \x -> expression.
\x -> x * 2 is an anonymous function that takes x and returns x * 2.
double = \x -> x * 2 is equivalent to double x = x * 2, but written as a lambda.

title: Where Clauses
learning_objective: Use a where clause to give local names to intermediate values.
stub_code:
module WhereClause where

hypotenuse :: Double -> Double -> Double
hypotenuse a b = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import WhereClause

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "hypotenuse 3 4" (hypotenuse 3 4) 5.0
    , assertEqual "hypotenuse 5 12" (hypotenuse 5 12) 13.0
    , assertEqual "hypotenuse 0 0" (hypotenuse 0 0) 0.0
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module WhereClause where

hypotenuse :: Double -> Double -> Double
hypotenuse a b = sqrt (aSquared + bSquared)
  where
    aSquared = a * a
    bSquared = b * b
hints: where introduces local bindings after the function body.
Define aSquared = a * a and bSquared = b * b in the where clause.
sqrt computes the square root of a Double. Use it on the sum of the two squared values.

title: Let Expressions
learning_objective: Use a let...in expression to bind local names inside an expression.
stub_code:
module LetExpression where

-- Use a let expression to compute the area, then double it
doubledArea :: Int -> Int -> Int
doubledArea w h = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import LetExpression

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "doubledArea 3 4" (doubledArea 3 4) 24
    , assertEqual "doubledArea 1 1" (doubledArea 1 1) 2
    , assertEqual "doubledArea 5 0" (doubledArea 5 0) 0
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module LetExpression where

doubledArea :: Int -> Int -> Int
doubledArea w h =
  let area = w * h
  in  area * 2
hints: let <bindings> in <expression> introduces local names scoped to that expression.
Compute the area first: let area = w * h
Then use it: in area * 2

title: Function Composition
learning_objective: Combine two functions into one using the . composition operator.
stub_code:
module FunctionComposition where

-- Compose negate and abs so the result is always non-positive
absoluteThenNegate :: Int -> Int
absoluteThenNegate = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import FunctionComposition

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "absoluteThenNegate 5" (absoluteThenNegate 5) (-5)
    , assertEqual "absoluteThenNegate (-3)" (absoluteThenNegate (-3)) (-3)
    , assertEqual "absoluteThenNegate 0" (absoluteThenNegate 0) 0
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module FunctionComposition where

absoluteThenNegate :: Int -> Int
absoluteThenNegate = negate . abs
hints: (f . g) x means f (g x) — g is applied first, then f.
negate flips the sign of a number; abs returns its absolute value.
negate . abs is a function that applies abs first, then negate.

title: Partial Application
learning_objective: Create a new function by partially applying an existing function.
stub_code:
module PartialApplication where

-- Implement using partial application of (+)
addFive :: Int -> Int
addFive = undefined

-- Implement using partial application of (*)
multiplyByThree :: Int -> Int
multiplyByThree = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import PartialApplication

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "addFive 3" (addFive 3) 8
    , assertEqual "addFive 0" (addFive 0) 5
    , assertEqual "multiplyByThree 4" (multiplyByThree 4) 12
    , assertEqual "multiplyByThree 0" (multiplyByThree 0) 0
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module PartialApplication where

addFive :: Int -> Int
addFive = (+5)

multiplyByThree :: Int -> Int
multiplyByThree = (*3)
hints: In Haskell, applying a function to fewer arguments than it expects returns a new function.
(+5) is an operator section — shorthand for \x -> x + 5.
(*3) does the same for multiplication: \x -> x * 3.

title: List Basics
learning_objective: Access list elements using head and tail.
stub_code:
module ListBasics where

-- Return the second element of a non-empty list.
secondElement :: [Int] -> Int
secondElement xs = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import ListBasics

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "returns 2 from [1,2,3]" (secondElement [1,2,3]) (2)
    , assertEqual "returns 20 from [10,20,30]" (secondElement [10,20,30]) (20)
    , assertEqual "returns the second of a two-element list" (secondElement [7,8]) (8)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module ListBasics where

secondElement :: [Int] -> Int
secondElement xs = head (tail xs)
hints: head returns the first element of a list; tail returns everything after the first element.
tail [1,2,3] gives [2,3]. What does head give you from that?
Combine head and tail: apply tail to get the list starting at the second element, then head to get the first of that.

title: List Ranges
learning_objective: Create a list of integers using Haskell range syntax.
stub_code:
module ListRanges where

-- Return a list of integers from 1 to n inclusive.
oneToN :: Int -> [Int]
oneToN n = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import ListRanges

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "returns [1..5] for n=5" (oneToN 5) ([1,2,3,4,5])
    , assertEqual "returns [1] for n=1" (oneToN 1) ([1])
    , assertEqual "returns [] for n=0" (oneToN 0) ([])
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module ListRanges where

oneToN :: Int -> [Int]
oneToN n = [1..n]
hints: Haskell range syntax [a..b] generates a list from a to b inclusive.
You can use a variable in a range expression.
[1..n] produces the list [1, 2, 3, ..., n]. If n < 1 it produces [].

title: Building Lists
learning_objective: Prepend an element to a list using the (:) cons operator.
stub_code:
module ListConstruction where

prepend :: Int -> [Int] -> [Int]
prepend x xs = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import ListConstruction

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "prepends to a non-empty list" (prepend 1 [2,3,4]) ([1,2,3,4])
    , assertEqual "prepends to an empty list" (prepend 5 []) ([5])
    , assertEqual "prepends 0 to a list" (prepend 0 [1,2,3]) ([0,1,2,3])
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module ListConstruction where

prepend :: Int -> [Int] -> [Int]
prepend x xs = x : xs
hints: The : operator (pronounced 'cons') prepends a single element to a list.
x : xs puts x at the front of the list xs.
Note that : takes an element on the left and a list on the right — unlike ++ which takes two lists.

title: Mapping Over a List
learning_objective: Apply a function to every element of a list using map.
stub_code:
module MapFunction where

doubleAll :: [Int] -> [Int]
doubleAll xs = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import MapFunction

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "doubles every element" (doubleAll [1,2,3]) ([2,4,6])
    , assertEqual "works on an empty list" (doubleAll []) ([])
    , assertEqual "doubles a single-element list" (doubleAll [7]) ([14])
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module MapFunction where

doubleAll :: [Int] -> [Int]
doubleAll xs = map (*2) xs
hints: map applies a function to every element of a list: map f [a,b,c] gives [f a, f b, f c].
(*2) is a section — a partially applied operator. (*2) 5 gives 10.
map (*2) applies doubling to every element. Try writing it as: map (*2) xs.

title: Filtering a List
learning_objective: Select elements satisfying a predicate using filter.
stub_code:
module FilterFunction where

evensOnly :: [Int] -> [Int]
evensOnly xs = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import FilterFunction

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "keeps only even numbers" (evensOnly [1,2,3,4,5,6]) ([2,4,6])
    , assertEqual "returns empty for all-odd input" (evensOnly [1,3,5]) ([])
    , assertEqual "returns all for all-even input" (evensOnly [2,4,6]) ([2,4,6])
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module FilterFunction where

evensOnly :: [Int] -> [Int]
evensOnly xs = filter even xs
hints: filter keeps only the elements for which a predicate function returns True.
The type of filter is (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> [a].
Haskell's Prelude includes even :: Int -> Bool, which returns True for even numbers.

title: Type Signatures
learning_objective: Write an explicit type signature for a function.
stub_code:
module TypeSignatures where

-- Add a type signature above this function.
-- It takes two Strings and returns one String.
joinWithSpace x y = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import TypeSignatures

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "joins two words" (joinWithSpace "hello" "world") ("hello world")
    , assertEqual "joins with a space in between" (joinWithSpace "foo" "bar") ("foo bar")
    , assertEqual "joins with empty string" (joinWithSpace "" "bar") (" bar")
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module TypeSignatures where

joinWithSpace :: String -> String -> String
joinWithSpace x y = x ++ " " ++ y
hints: A type signature is written on the line above the function: functionName :: Type.
Arrow -> separates argument types and the return type: String -> String -> String.
This function takes two Strings and returns a String — join them with a space using ++.

title: The Maybe Type
learning_objective: Return a Maybe value using the Just and Nothing constructors.
stub_code:
module MaybeType where

-- Return Just the first element, or Nothing if the list is empty.
safeHead :: [Int] -> Maybe Int
safeHead xs = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import MaybeType

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "returns Just the first element" (safeHead [1,2,3]) (Just 1)
    , assertEqual "returns Nothing for empty list" (safeHead []) (Nothing)
    , assertEqual "returns Just for a single-element list" (safeHead [42]) (Just 42)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module MaybeType where

safeHead :: [Int] -> Maybe Int
safeHead [] = Nothing
safeHead (x:_) = Just x
hints: Maybe represents an optional value. Just x wraps a value; Nothing represents absence.
Pattern match on the list: the empty list case [] and the non-empty case (x:xs).
Return Nothing for [] and Just x for (x:_). The _ ignores the tail.

title: Maybe with a Default
learning_objective: Extract a value from a Maybe, supplying a default for the Nothing case.
stub_code:

import Data.Maybe (fromMaybe)

-- Return the Int inside the Maybe, or 0 if it is Nothing.
orZero :: Maybe Int -> Int
orZero m = undefined

module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)

assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "returns the value inside Just" (orZero (Just 5)) (5)
    , assertEqual "returns 0 for Nothing" (orZero Nothing) (0)
    , assertEqual "returns the value for Just 0" (orZero (Just 0)) (0)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module MaybeDefault where

import Data.Maybe (fromMaybe)

orZero :: Maybe Int -> Int
orZero m = fromMaybe 0 m
hints: fromMaybe takes a default value and a Maybe: fromMaybe default (Just x) gives x, fromMaybe default Nothing gives default.
The import for fromMaybe is already provided at the top of the file.
fromMaybe 0 applied to your Maybe Int gives either the wrapped value or 0.

title: Tuples
learning_objective: Construct and destructure a 2-tuple by pattern matching.
stub_code:
module Tuples where

-- Swap the two elements of a pair.
swap :: (a, b) -> (b, a)
swap pair = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import Tuples

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "swaps an Int and a String" (swap (1 :: Int, "hello")) (("hello", 1))
    , assertEqual "swaps two Strings" (swap ("a", "b")) (("b", "a"))
    , assertEqual "swaps identical types" (swap (True, False)) ((False, True))
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module Tuples where

swap :: (a, b) -> (b, a)
swap (x, y) = (y, x)
hints: A tuple groups values of potentially different types: (Int, String) is a valid type.
You can pattern match on the pair directly in the argument: swap (x, y) = ...
Build the result by reversing the order: (y, x).

title: Custom Data Types
learning_objective: Define a sum type with data and pattern match on its constructors.
stub_code:
module CustomDataTypes where

data Direction = North | South | East | West

-- Return the opposite direction.
opposite :: Direction -> Direction
opposite dir = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import CustomDataTypes

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "North is opposite of South" (opposite North) (South)
    , assertEqual "South is opposite of North" (opposite South) (North)
    , assertEqual "East is opposite of West" (opposite East) (West)
    , assertEqual "West is opposite of East" (opposite West) (East)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module CustomDataTypes where

data Direction = North | South | East | West deriving (Eq, Show)

opposite :: Direction -> Direction
opposite North = South
opposite South = North
opposite East  = West
opposite West  = East
hints: data defines a new type. Each option separated by | is a constructor: data Color = Red | Blue.
You can pattern match on constructors the same way you match on literals.
Write one equation per constructor — the compiler will warn if you miss one.

title: Pattern Matching on Lists
learning_objective: Pattern match on list structure using [], [x], and (x:xs).
stub_code:
module PatternMatchingLists where

-- Return True only if the list contains exactly one element.
isSingleton :: [a] -> Bool
isSingleton xs = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import PatternMatchingLists

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "returns True for a one-element list" (isSingleton [42 :: Int]) (True)
    , assertEqual "returns False for an empty list" (isSingleton ([] :: [Int])) (False)
    , assertEqual "returns False for a two-element list" (isSingleton [1 :: Int, 2]) (False)
    , assertEqual "returns False for a longer list" (isSingleton [1 :: Int, 2, 3]) (False)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module PatternMatchingLists where

isSingleton :: [a] -> Bool
isSingleton [_] = True
isSingleton _   = False
hints: List patterns: [] matches empty, [x] matches exactly one element, (x:xs) matches one or more.
The pattern [_] matches any list with exactly one element — _ ignores the value.
Use a catch-all _ pattern for the False case.

title: Pattern Matching on Maybe
learning_objective: Pattern match on Just and Nothing to transform a Maybe value.
stub_code:
module PatternMatchingMaybe where

-- Add 1 to the value inside the Maybe, or return Nothing.
incrementMaybe :: Maybe Int -> Maybe Int
incrementMaybe m = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import PatternMatchingMaybe

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "increments Just 5 to Just 6" (incrementMaybe (Just 5)) (Just 6)
    , assertEqual "returns Nothing for Nothing" (incrementMaybe Nothing) (Nothing)
    , assertEqual "increments Just 0 to Just 1" (incrementMaybe (Just 0)) (Just 1)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module PatternMatchingMaybe where

incrementMaybe :: Maybe Int -> Maybe Int
incrementMaybe Nothing  = Nothing
incrementMaybe (Just n) = Just (n + 1)
hints: Maybe has two constructors: Nothing and Just x. Write one equation for each.
To match on Just and bind its inner value: Just n matches a Just and binds the Int to n.
For the Just case, return Just (n + 1). For Nothing, return Nothing.

title: Case Expressions
learning_objective: Use a case expression to branch on a value inside a function body.
stub_code:
module CaseExpressions where

-- Return "negative", "zero", or "positive" depending on the sign of n.
describe :: Int -> String
describe n = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import CaseExpressions

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "describes a positive number" (CaseExpressions.describe 5) ("positive")
    , assertEqual "describes zero" (CaseExpressions.describe 0) ("zero")
    , assertEqual "describes a negative number" (CaseExpressions.describe (-3)) ("negative")
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module CaseExpressions where

describe :: Int -> String
describe n = case compare n 0 of
  LT -> "negative"
  EQ -> "zero"
  GT -> "positive"
hints: case <expr> of { pattern -> result; ... } lets you branch on any value.
compare a b returns an Ordering: LT (less than), EQ (equal), or GT (greater than).
You can also solve this with if/then/else or guards — but case on compare is idiomatic.

title: Guards
learning_objective: Use guards as a readable alternative to chained if/then/else.
stub_code:
module Guards where

-- Return the letter grade for a numeric score (0-100).
-- A: 90-100, B: 80-89, C: 70-79, D: 60-69, F: below 60
letterGrade :: Int -> String
letterGrade score = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import Guards

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "returns A for 95" (letterGrade 95) ("A")
    , assertEqual "returns B for 85" (letterGrade 85) ("B")
    , assertEqual "returns C for 75" (letterGrade 75) ("C")
    , assertEqual "returns D for 65" (letterGrade 65) ("D")
    , assertEqual "returns F for 55" (letterGrade 55) ("F")
    , assertEqual "returns A for 90" (letterGrade 90) ("A")
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module Guards where

letterGrade :: Int -> String
letterGrade score
  | score >= 90 = "A"
  | score >= 80 = "B"
  | score >= 70 = "C"
  | score >= 60 = "D"
  | otherwise   = "F"
hints: Guards follow the function arguments using | condition = result syntax.
Guards are checked top to bottom; the first one that is True wins.
otherwise is just a synonym for True and acts as the catch-all at the end.

title: Pattern Matching on Tuples
learning_objective: Destructure a tuple argument using pattern matching.
stub_code:
module PatternMatchingTuples where

-- Add the two integers in a pair.
addPair :: (Int, Int) -> Int
addPair pair = undefined
hidden_test_suite:

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import PatternMatchingTuples

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "adds (1, 2)" (addPair (1, 2)) (3)

canonical_solution:
module PatternMatchingTuples where

addPair :: (Int, Int) -> Int
addPair (x, y) = x + y
hints: You can pattern match on a tuple directly in the function argument.
addPair (x, y) = ... binds the first element to x and the second to y.
Once you have x and y, just add them.

title: Recursive Factorial
learning_objective: Implement a recursive function with a base case and a recursive case.
stub_code:
module RecursiveFactorial where

-- Compute n! recursively. Assume n >= 0.
factorial :: Integer -> Integer
factorial n = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import RecursiveFactorial

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "factorial 0 = 1" (factorial 0) (1)
    , assertEqual "factorial 1 = 1" (factorial 1) (1)
    , assertEqual "factorial 5 = 120" (factorial 5) (120)
    , assertEqual "factorial 10 = 3628800" (factorial 10) (3628800)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module RecursiveFactorial where

factorial :: Integer -> Integer
factorial 0 = 1
factorial n = n * factorial (n - 1)
hints: A recursive function needs a base case (to stop) and a recursive case (to call itself).
The base case: factorial 0 = 1.
The recursive case: n! = n × (n-1)!  Write it as: factorial n = n * factorial (n - 1).

title: Recursive Sum
learning_objective: Recurse over a list by pattern matching on [] and (x:xs).
stub_code:
module RecursiveSum where

-- Sum a list of integers using recursion. Do not use the built-in sum.
mySum :: [Int] -> Int
mySum xs = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import RecursiveSum

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "sums an empty list to 0" (mySum []) (0)
    , assertEqual "sums [1,2,3] to 6" (mySum [1,2,3]) (6)
    , assertEqual "sums [10,20,30] to 60" (mySum [10,20,30]) (60)
    , assertEqual "sums a single-element list" (mySum [7]) (7)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module RecursiveSum where

mySum :: [Int] -> Int
mySum []     = 0
mySum (x:xs) = x + mySum xs
hints: Pattern match on the list: [] for the empty case, (x:xs) for the non-empty case.
The sum of an empty list is 0 — that is the base case.
The sum of (x:xs) is x plus the sum of xs. Call mySum recursively on xs.

title: Recursive Length
learning_objective: Compute the length of a list using recursion.
stub_code:
module RecursiveLength where

-- Return the length of a list. Do not use the built-in length.
myLength :: [a] -> Int
myLength xs = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import RecursiveLength

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "empty list has length 0" (myLength ([] :: [Int])) (0)
    , assertEqual "[1,2,3] has length 3" (myLength [1 :: Int, 2, 3]) (3)
    , assertEqual "works on a list of strings" (myLength ["a", "b", "c", "d"]) (4)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module RecursiveLength where

myLength :: [a] -> Int
myLength []     = 0
myLength (_:xs) = 1 + myLength xs
hints: The length of an empty list is 0.
The length of a non-empty list is 1 plus the length of its tail.
You don't need the head value — use _ to ignore it: (_:xs).

title: Recursive Reverse
learning_objective: Reverse a list using recursion and list concatenation.
stub_code:
module RecursiveReverse where

-- Reverse a list using recursion. Do not use the built-in reverse.
myReverse :: [a] -> [a]
myReverse xs = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import RecursiveReverse

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "reverses an empty list" (myReverse ([] :: [Int])) ([])
    , assertEqual "reverses [1,2,3]" (myReverse [1 :: Int, 2, 3]) ([3, 2, 1])
    , assertEqual "reverses a single-element list" (myReverse [42 :: Int]) ([42])
    , assertEqual "reverses a string list" (myReverse ["a", "b", "c"]) (["c", "b", "a"])
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module RecursiveReverse where

myReverse :: [a] -> [a]
myReverse []     = []
myReverse (x:xs) = myReverse xs ++ [x]
hints: The reverse of an empty list is an empty list.
To reverse (x:xs), reverse xs first and then put x at the end.
To put x at the end, append the single-element list [x] using ++.

title: Tail Recursion with an Accumulator
learning_objective: Use an accumulator argument to write a tail-recursive function.
stub_code:
module TailRecursion where

-- Reverse a list using a tail-recursive helper with an accumulator.
myReverse :: [a] -> [a]
myReverse xs = go xs []
  where
    go remaining acc = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import TailRecursion

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "reverses an empty list" (myReverse ([] :: [Int])) ([])
    , assertEqual "reverses [1,2,3]" (myReverse [1 :: Int, 2, 3]) ([3, 2, 1])
    , assertEqual "reverses a long list" (myReverse [1..100 :: Int]) ([100,99..1])
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module TailRecursion where

myReverse :: [a] -> [a]
myReverse xs = go xs []
  where
    go []     acc = acc
    go (x:xs) acc = go xs (x:acc)
hints: The accumulator starts empty. Each recursive step moves the head of remaining into acc.
When remaining is empty, the accumulator holds the reversed list — return it.
go (x:xs) acc should call go xs (x:acc) — prepend x to acc and recurse on the tail.

title: Deriving Show
learning_objective: Add a deriving clause to make a custom type printable via show.
stub_code:
module DerivingShow where

-- Add `deriving (Show)` to this data declaration.
data Color = Red | Green | Blue

colorName :: Color -> String
colorName c = show c
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import DerivingShow

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "shows Red" (colorName Red) ("Red")
    , assertEqual "shows Green" (colorName Green) ("Green")
    , assertEqual "shows Blue" (colorName Blue) ("Blue")
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module DerivingShow where

data Color = Red | Green | Blue deriving (Show)

colorName :: Color -> String
colorName c = show c
hints: show converts a value to a String, but it requires the type to have a Show instance.
The easiest way to get a Show instance is deriving: add deriving (Show) after the constructors.
Change the data declaration to: data Color = Red | Green | Blue deriving (Show)

title: Deriving Eq
learning_objective: Derive Eq to enable == on a custom type.
stub_code:
module DerivingEq where

-- Add `deriving (Eq)` to enable equality comparison.
data Suit = Clubs | Diamonds | Hearts | Spades

sameSuit :: Suit -> Suit -> Bool
sameSuit a b = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import DerivingEq

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "Clubs equals Clubs" (sameSuit Clubs Clubs) (True)
    , assertEqual "Clubs does not equal Hearts" (sameSuit Clubs Hearts) (False)
    , assertEqual "Spades equals Spades" (sameSuit Spades Spades) (True)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module DerivingEq where

data Suit = Clubs | Diamonds | Hearts | Spades deriving (Eq)

sameSuit :: Suit -> Suit -> Bool
sameSuit a b = a == b
hints: The == operator requires the type to have an Eq instance.
Add deriving (Eq) to the data declaration to get == for free.
Once Eq is derived, sameSuit a b = a == b works directly.

title: Implementing Show
learning_objective: Write a manual Show instance to control how a type is displayed.
stub_code:

data Suit = Clubs | Diamonds | Hearts | Spades

instance Show Suit where
  show suit = undefined

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import ImplementingShow

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "shows Clubs as the club symbol" (show Clubs) ("\9827")
    , assertEqual "shows Diamonds as the diamond symbol" (show Diamonds) ("\9830")
    , assertEqual "shows Hearts as the heart symbol" (show Hearts) ("\9829")
    , assertEqual "shows Spades as the spade symbol" (show Spades) ("\9824")

  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module ImplementingShow where

data Suit = Clubs | Diamonds | Hearts | Spades

instance Show Suit where
  show Clubs    = "\9827"
  show Diamonds = "\9830"
  show Hearts   = "\9829"
  show Spades   = "\9824"
hints: instance Show Suit where lets you define how show converts a Suit to a String.
Pattern match on each constructor inside the show function body.

title: Implementing Eq
learning_objective: Write a manual Eq instance by implementing ==.
stub_code:
module ImplementingEq where

data Point = Point Int Int

-- Two Points are equal if both coordinates match.
instance Eq Point where
  (==) a b = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import ImplementingEq

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "equal points are equal" (Point 1 2 == Point 1 2) (True)
    , assertEqual "different x coordinates are not equal" (Point 1 2 == Point 3 2) (False)
    , assertEqual "different y coordinates are not equal" (Point 1 2 == Point 1 3) (False)
    , assertEqual "unequal points satisfy /=" (Point 0 0 /= Point 1 1) (True)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module ImplementingEq where

data Point = Point Int Int

instance Eq Point where
  (==) (Point x1 y1) (Point x2 y2) = x1 == x2 && y1 == y2
hints: Implement == by pattern matching on both Point values to extract their coordinates.
(==) (Point x1 y1) (Point x2 y2) = ... binds both points' fields.
Two Points are equal if x1 == x2 AND y1 == y2. Haskell derives /= automatically from ==.

title: fmap on Maybe
learning_objective: Use fmap to apply a function inside a Maybe without pattern matching.
stub_code:
module FmapMaybe where

-- Double the value inside a Maybe Int.
-- Return Nothing if the input is Nothing.
-- Use fmap rather than pattern matching.
doubleIfPresent :: Maybe Int -> Maybe Int
doubleIfPresent m = undefined
hidden_test_suite:
module Main where

import System.Exit (exitFailure, exitSuccess)
import FmapMaybe

assertEqual :: (Show a, Eq a) => String -> a -> a -> IO Bool
assertEqual lbl got want
  | got == want = do
      putStrLn $ "  PASS: " ++ lbl
      return True
  | otherwise = do
      putStrLn $ "  FAIL: " ++ lbl
      putStrLn $ "    expected: " ++ show want
      putStrLn $ "    got:      " ++ show got
      return False

main :: IO ()
main = do
  results <- sequence
    [ assertEqual "doubles Just 5 to Just 10" (doubleIfPresent (Just 5)) (Just 10)
    , assertEqual "returns Nothing for Nothing" (doubleIfPresent Nothing) (Nothing)
    , assertEqual "doubles Just 0 to Just 0" (doubleIfPresent (Just 0)) (Just 0)
    ]
  let passed = length (filter id results)
      failed  = length results - passed
  putStrLn $ show (length results) ++ " examples, " ++ show failed ++ " failures"
  if failed == 0 then exitSuccess else exitFailure
canonical_solution:
module FmapMaybe where

doubleIfPresent :: Maybe Int -> Maybe Int
doubleIfPresent m = fmap (*2) m
hints: fmap applies a function to the value inside a functor without unwrapping it.
fmap f (Just x) = Just (f x) and fmap f Nothing = Nothing.
fmap (*2) applies the doubling function. Pass your Maybe as the second argument.
